title: Stronger Cell Probe Lower Bounds via Local PRGs
speaker: Oliver Korten
abstract:
In this work, we develop a new method for proving lower bounds for static data structures in the classical cellprobe model of Yao. Our methods give the strongest known lower bounds for any explicit problem in this model (quadratically stronger for space as a function of time) and break a barrier which has stood for a few decades. Our lower bounds are based on a new connection we establish between the static cell probe model and NC0 generators, which have been studied extensively in cryptography and more recently in the context of “range avoidance.
Joint with Toniann Pitassi (Columbia University) and Russell Impagliazzo (UC San Diego).

proof_stages.label: Static cell-probe setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk formalizes static data-structure problems as Boolean query-by-data matrices and defines space, time, word length, bit-probe versus cell-probe access, and the basic explicit-lower-bound goal.

proof_stages.label: Prior lower bounds and announced improvements
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews communication-complexity and Siegel cell-sampling lower bounds, the special nonadaptive bit-probe bounds, and states the new quadratic improvements for k-wise independent and small-bias data-structure problems.

proof_stages.label: Local generators and range avoidance
proof_stages.summary: NC0_t generators are introduced as stretch maps whose outputs each depend on t inputs, together with the two central tasks of distinguishing their outputs from uniform and finding strings outside their range.

proof_stages.label: Equivalence with range-avoidance hitting sets
proof_stages.summary: A direct construction turns any nonadaptive bit-probe data structure into an NC0_t generator whose range contains all columns of the data-structure matrix, yielding an equivalence between lower bounds and short hitting lists for range avoidance, plus related algorithmic and barrier consequences.

proof_stages.label: Semirandom CSP refutation strategy
proof_stages.summary: Membership of a string in a local generator range is encoded as a t-CSP with worst-case left-hand sides and random right-hand sides, and the goal becomes showing that refutation algorithms still certify unsatisfiability when the right-hand side is drawn from a pseudorandom column distribution.

proof_stages.label: Spectral certificates under limited independence
proof_stages.summary: The refutation algorithms are abstracted as signed sums of matrices whose spectral norm is large on generator outputs but small for random signs, and this smallness is transferred to k-wise or almost k-wise independent signs via low-degree trace-polynomial approximations.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic reduction and Kikuchi matrices
proof_stages.summary: The required matrices arise by blocking a t-CSP into a 2-CSP over larger meta-variables, or more generally by the Kikuchi matrix method, which explains the s roughly at most n^(2/t) parameter regime and the even-t reduction.

proof_stages.label: Frontier and open problems beyond the CSP barrier
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion frames the new lower bounds as catching data structures up to the random-CSP refutation frontier, discusses natural-proofs and circuit-lower-bound barriers, and suggests lifting lower bounds from related query or communication models as a possible route forward.

examples.name: Biased or OR-output local predicates
examples.note: In response to a question, biased predicates such as OR outputs illustrate that some statistical attacks on NC0 PRGs need not yield useful range-refutation certificates.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through biased predicates such as OR outputs by applying the simple bias distinguisher and noting why this does not similarly yield refutation
